subsections.title: JUnit 5 Extended
subsections.keyRule: JUnit 5 advanced: @Disabled=skip. @DisplayName=custom test name. @Nested=grouped tests with context. @ParameterizedTest=data-driven. @RepeatedTest=run N times. @Tag=filtering. Assumptions=skip on condition. assertThrows/assertAll/assertTimeout for special assertions.

terms.what: @ParameterizedTest runs the same test with different inputs. Sources: @ValueSource (literals), @CsvSource (CSV rows), @MethodSource (method returning stream), @EnumSource, @NullAndEmptySource.
terms.why: Test boundary conditions, multiple inputs, edge cases without copy-pasting test methods.
terms.how: @ParameterizedTest @ValueSource(ints={0,-1,Integer.MAX_VALUE}) void testEdgeCases(int input) {...} @ParameterizedTest @CsvSource({'Alice,25','Bob,30'}) void test(String name, int age) {...}
terms.where: Validation logic, boundary testing, format parsing, calculation tests.
terms.when: Use when you need to test same logic with many inputs. Reduces test boilerplate significantly.
terms.issue: @MethodSource method must be static (unless @TestInstance(PER_CLASS)). Forgetting static = test fails to run.
terms.fix: Make @MethodSource methods static: static Stream<Arguments> dataProvider() { ... }

star.s: 10 copy-paste tests for email validation with different inputs.
star.r: One test method, 10 cases. Failures show which input fails.

terms.what: assertThrows: verifies exception thrown — returns exception for further checks. assertAll: runs all assertions regardless of failures — sees all failures. assertTimeout: verifies method completes within time.
terms.why: assertThrows replaces try-catch in tests. assertAll shows ALL failures instead of stopping at first. assertTimeout catches slow methods.
terms.how: Exception ex = assertThrows(IllegalArgumentException.class, () -> service.create(null)); assertEquals('Name required', ex.getMessage()); assertAll('user', () - > assertEquals(..), () -> assertNotNull(..));
terms.where: Negative test cases, testing validation, performance assertions, comprehensive data validation.
terms.when: Use assertThrows for negative tests. assertAll when validating multiple fields of a result object. assertTimeout for SLA-critical methods.

terms.what: @RepeatedTest(n): run same test n times — for flakiness detection or statistical confidence. @Tag: label tests for filtering (run only 'fast' or 'slow' or 'integration'). Assumptions.assumeTrue(): skip test if condition not met.
terms.why: @RepeatedTest finds intermittent failures. @Tag enables selective test runs (CI runs 'fast' tests on every commit, 'slow' tests nightly). Assumptions skip tests gracefully.
terms.how: @RepeatedTest(5) void testIdempotent(). @Tag('slow') @Test void testLargeFile(). Assumptions.assumeTrue(System.getenv('DB_URL') != null, 'DB not configured — skip');
terms.where: @Tag for test suites. @RepeatedTest for concurrency tests. Assumptions for environment-dependent tests.
terms.when: Use @Tag to separate fast unit tests from slow integration tests. Use Assumptions when test only makes sense in certain environments.
terms.issue: @Tag not configured in Maven Surefire — tags are added but tests still all run or all skip.

star.s: CI builds take 15 minutes — slow integration tests run on every commit.
star.a: @Tag('integration') on slow tests. CI profile runs only @Tag('unit'). Nightly run runs all.

terms.what: @Spy wraps a real object — real methods called unless explicitly stubbed. @Captor captures method argument values for verification.
terms.why: Spy when you want to test a real class but stub only some methods. Captor when you need to verify the exact argument passed to a mock.
terms.how: @Spy UserService userService = new UserService(); doReturn('mocked').when(userService).expensiveMethod(); @Captor ArgumentCaptor<Order> orderCaptor; verify(repo).save(orderCaptor.capture()); assertEquals(100, orderCaptor.getValue().getAmount());
terms.where: Testing classes with one or two methods that need stubbing. Verifying exact argument content in mock calls.
terms.when: Use @Spy when most real behavior is wanted but specific methods need mocking. Use @Captor instead of ArgumentCaptor.forClass() — cleaner.
terms.issue: Spy on a real object — spy created via constructor but tested via new call — spy not active.
terms.fix: Always use @Spy with @ExtendWith(MockitoExtension.class). Or manually: UserService spy = Mockito.spy(new UserService());

star.s: Need to test that correct Order object is passed to repository.save().
star.a: ArgumentCaptor<Order> cap = ArgumentCaptor.forClass(Order.class); verify(repo).save(cap.capture()); assertEquals(expected, cap.getValue()).

terms.what: doReturn(value).when(spy).method() — stubs without calling real method. doThrow(ex).when(spy).method() — throws on spy method call. Safer than when().thenReturn() for spies.

InOrder verifies mock interactions in a specific order. verify(mock, times(n)) verifies a method was called exactly n times. verify(mock, never()) = called 0 times. verify(mock, atLeast(n)) = at least n times.
terms.why: Some business logic requires operations in a specific sequence. Verifying call count catches under/over-calling.
terms.how: InOrder inOrder = inOrder(mock1, mock2); inOrder.verify(mock1).authenticate(); inOrder.verify(mock2).process(); verify(emailService, times(1)).send(any());
terms.where: Protocol-sensitive operations (auth before process), audit trails, idempotency testing.
terms.when: Use InOrder when sequence matters. Use times()/never() when call count matters for correctness.
terms.issue: verify() with no count spec = times(1). Easy to forget — test passes even if method called twice.
terms.fix: Be explicit: verify(service, times(1)).send(any()); or verify(service, never()).send(any()); — clear intent.

star.s: Email service called twice on one order — duplicate emails sent.
star.a: verify(emailService, times(1)).send(any()). Test fails if called twice.

terms.what: @MockBean replaces a Spring bean with a Mockito mock in the ApplicationContext. @SpyBean wraps a real Spring bean with a Mockito spy. Both work in @SpringBootTest and @WebMvcTest.
terms.why: @Mock only works in unit tests without Spring context. @MockBean replaces beans in the full Spring context — for integration tests.
terms.how: @MockBean UserRepository userRepo; // replaces real UserRepository in context. @SpyBean UserService userService; // wraps real UserService.
terms.where: @SpringBootTest integration tests, @WebMvcTest controller tests with mocked service.
terms.when: Use @MockBean to avoid hitting real DB in integration tests. Use @SpyBean when you want mostly real behavior but need to stub specific methods.
terms.issue: @MockBean recreates the ApplicationContext for each test class — slow if many test classes use different @MockBean combinations.
terms.fix: Group tests with same @MockBean setup in one test class. Use @MockitoSettings + @ExtendWith(MockitoExtension) for pure unit tests instead.

star.s: Controller test needs to mock service but use real Spring MVC layer.
star.a: @WebMvcTest + @MockBean UserService. Tests MVC layer with mocked service.

terms.what: WireMock is a mock HTTP server for testing. It simulates external HTTP services — REST APIs, third-party services. Tests call WireMock instead of real external service.
terms.why: External services are unreliable in tests: slow, rate-limited, may not have test data. WireMock is local, fast, controllable.
terms.where: Testing services that call external REST APIs, payment gateways, notification services.
terms.when: Use WireMock when your service calls external HTTP APIs and you want reliable, fast tests without real external calls.
terms.issue: WireMock stubs not matching real API — test passes but production breaks. Stubs become stale as real API evolves.
terms.fix: Record stubs from real API: WireMock recording mode. Validate stubs against API contracts (Pact contract testing).

star.s: Payment service tests are flaky — real payment gateway unreliable in CI.
star.a: WireMock simulates payment gateway. Predictable responses.
